title: Intel Ships Grantsdale and Alderwood Chipsets
author: Jay Lyman, TechNewsWorld
abstract: TechNewsWorld article (Jun 22 2004, Jay Lyman) on Intel's release of the 915 G/P (Grantsdale) and 925 X (Alderwood) desktop chipsets — bundled with new Pentium 4 LGA775 processors, supporting high-definition video, 7.1 surround audio, DDR2 memory, PCI Express, RAID 0/1, Wi-Fi, and hardware security for the forthcoming Windows XP SP2. Intel's Louis Burns calls this 'a significant evolution.' Aberdeen chief research officer Peter Kastner delivers the canonical analyst frame: 'The new 915 G/P and 925 X chipsets represent the biggest change in input-output capability in a decade.' Kastner says the primary benefit will be support for media-center-type computers, positively benefiting consumers and corporate users. Gartner's Martin Reynolds: 'A major cycle rather than a minor cycle… this is a big cycle and provides the platform headroom for new processors.' Reynolds forecasts the new chipsets will be the basis of most PCs for the next two years.
importance_rationale: Documents the Jun 2004 i915/i925 chipset launch — the platform that introduced PCI Express, DDR2, HD Audio, integrated graphics step, and LGA775 socket to mainstream Intel desktops. This chipset generation was the basis of most Intel PCs 2004-2007, exactly as Reynolds forecast. Kastner's 'biggest change in I/O in a decade' framing captures the platform significance.
relevance_rationale: The specific chipsets are historical, but PCI Express (introduced with Grantsdale) remains the standard PC expansion bus in 2025 (Gen5/6), DDR2's successor lineage continues to DDR5, HD Audio persists, and the integrated-graphics-for-media-center pattern extended to Intel's UHD/Iris Xe.
prescience_rationale: Kastner's 'biggest I/O change in a decade' framing proved correct — PCI Express replaced PCI/AGP, DDR2 replaced DDR, HD Audio replaced AC'97, all in this chipset generation. Reynolds' two-year-basis-of-most-PCs forecast held: i915/925 chipsets dominated 2004-2006 before Core 2 Duo (2006) and Intel 965 chipset. Media-center PCs (Windows XP MCE, Vista, 7) became mainstream 2005-2012 before streaming services displaced them.
